description:
Advances: mutagen.mutation_pipeline

Discovered during S7 (mutagen-wrd.9) end-to-end test driver authoring.

## Problem

`lib/mutagen_ex/mutation_runner/mutation_loop.ex` â€” per-site `Task.shutdown(task, :brutal_kill)` on a timed-out mutation aborts a task that may be mid-flight inside `:code.load_binary/3` (during the cover-recompile cycle), leaving the Code.Server with an unreleased load lock for the mutated module. Subsequent attempts to load any module in the same BEAM time out / deadlock waiting on the orphaned lock.

## Symptom

In the S7 end-to-end driver, Scenario 4 (`infinite_looper.ex` â†’ `:timeout`) cannot run in the same BEAM as the other scenarios. When it does run, scenarios that follow fail with hangs or with confused module-load errors. Scenarios 5/6/8 (per the original implementer's account) similarly suffer when the mutation-bearing scenarios precede them.

## Spec reference

`mutagen.mutation_pipeline` r4 (per-site timeout classification) + r7 (taint propagation across successors) â€” both rules require the BEAM stay healthy across timeout events.

## Resolution

Options to investigate:

1. Replace `Task.shutdown(:brutal_kill)` with a cooperative-cancellation path (e.g., a sentinel `receive` checkpoint inside the per-site sandbox, plus a graceful `exit(:timeout)`).
2. Run the per-site sandbox in a peer Erlang node so brutal_kill cannot touch the orchestrator's Code.Server. (Heavier â€” only if option 1 is not viable.)
3. After brutal_kill, explicitly release the Code.Server's per-module lock via the documented internal API (if one exists).

The fix must keep r4's timeout-classification semantics intact while not leaving the Code.Server poisoned for the next per-site iteration.

## Related

- mutagen-wrd.9 (S7 end-to-end suite, Scenario 4 + state isolation in driver)
- mutagen-wrd.11 / mutagen-wrd.12 (sister production-wire-up bugs surfaced in the same audit cycle)

- lib/mutagen_ex/mutation_runner.ex (post-:timeout :code.purge/1 settle pass)
- test/mutagen_ex/mutation_runner_test.exs (4 new tests, 2 new ExUnitFake actions)

  top-level warning count dropped 1 â†’ 0 because brutal_kill no longer leaks state)
- .spec/specs/mutation_pipeline.spec.md (r4 wording: "killed without grace" â†’ "cancelled via the two-phase path")
- .spec/decisions/timeout_handling.md (Amendment section + Decision/Consequences updated)

Tests added:
- MutationRunnerTest "graceful-cancel path: task that traps exit and returns on :shutdown classifies :timeout without brutal_kill"
- MutationRunnerTest "brutal-kill escalation: task that ignores :shutdown still terminates via :brutal_kill and classifies :timeout"
- MutationRunnerTest "code-server settle: :code.purge/1 is invoked for the site's scoped modules on :timeout"
- MutationRunnerTest "non-timeout outcomes do NOT call :code.purge/1 (settle is timeout-only)"

Verification stubs flipped: none (the existing v2 stub
`mix test test/mutagen_ex/mutation_runner_test.exs` already covers r4
and now also covers the new cooperative-cancel + settle path).

Test results:
- mix compile --warnings-as-errors: clean
- mix test (default suite): 309 tests, 0 failures, 1 doctest (was 305 â€” +4 new)
- mix test --include e2e_slow: 322 tests, 0 failures, 2 skipped
  - Scenario 4 (`infinite_looper.ex` â†’ `:timeout`) now passes alongside
    all other scenarios in the same BEAM. Multiple seeds verified.
  - Scenario 7 (`EctoUser`) stays `@tag :skip` â€” its blocker is
    cover-instrumentation-vs-DSL, not the Code.Server load-lock fix
    this ticket addresses.

Summary: replaced `Task.shutdown(:brutal_kill)` with a two-phase
cooperative cancel (trappable `:shutdown` first, default 100ms grace;
brutal_kill only on escalation) and added a `:code.purge/1` settle
pass on the site's scoped modules after every `:timeout` outcome.
Together these release the Code.Server's per-module load locks that
a brutal-killed task could orphan when mid-flight inside
`:code.load_binary/3`, fixing the BEAM-corruption pattern Scenario 4
exposed in the S7 e2e driver. r4 classification (`:timeout`) is
unchanged; the spec wording for the cancellation mechanism is
updated to match, with the durable rationale captured as an
Amendment in `mutagen.decision.timeout_handling`.

- Files matching Out-of-Scope globs: 0 (ticket declares none)

Semantic checks:
- Spec stubs run: 4, passed: 4
  - v1 mix test test/mutagen_ex/baseline_test.exs â€” 8 tests, 0 failures
  - v2 mix test test/mutagen_ex/mutation_runner_test.exs â€” 22 tests, 0 failures
  - v3 mix test test/mutagen_ex/integration/c1_test.exs â€” 1 test, 0 failures
  - v4 mix test test/mutagen_ex/integration/c2_test.exs â€” 1 test, 0 failures
- Requirements coverage (must rules â€” focusing on those the diff touches; r1/r2/r3/r8/r9/r10/r11 covered by their existing stubs which still pass):
  - r4: covered by test/mutagen_ex/mutation_runner_test.exs:485 (timeout taint), :541 (graceful-cancel honoured), :584 (brutal-kill escalation with elapsed_ms<800 ceiling), :615 (purge invoked on :timeout). Would fail if the two-phase cancel reverted to brutal-only (test :541 would no longer reach `:survived` via the graceful exit branch) OR if `:code.purge/1` settle was dropped (test :615 `scope_module in purged` assertion would fail).
  - r5: covered by mutation_runner_test.exs:423 (:killed), :437 (:survived), :450 (:compile_error), :468 (:error), :485 (:timeout). Would fail if classify/3 mapped any of the five outcomes to the wrong status.
  - r6: covered by mutation_runner_test.exs:834 (restore call uses original AST) and :853 (unrecoverable_restore_failure). Would fail if restore was skipped on the new settle path, or if the unrecoverable_restore_failure abort was suppressed.
  - r7: covered by mutation_runner_test.exs:485 (taint via :timeout) and :715 (snapshot growth without timeout). Would fail if `next_tainted = tainted_now or grew? or status == :timeout` dropped the `grew?` term, or if snapshot_warning/2 stopped emitting on grew?.
  - r1, r2: covered by baseline_test.exs (unchanged; v1 passes).
  - r3: covered by mutation_runner_test.exs:371 (self-mutation refusal scope match).
  - r8: covered by mutation_runner_test.exs:883 (state_drift_warning on `use GenServer`).
  - r9: covered by mutation_runner_test.exs:943 (compiler stderr â†’ results[i].warnings).
  - r10: covered by integration/c2_test.exs.
  - r11: covered by mutation_runner_test.exs:972 (sha256 lib/ files pre/post).
- Intent-level scope concerns: none. Ticket required "keep r4's timeout-classification semantics intact while not leaving the Code.Server poisoned"; the diff does both. The spec r4 amendment was explicit and limited to the cancellation mechanism. No unrelated subsystems touched.
- Project verification: pass.
  - mix compile --warnings-as-errors â†’ exit 0
  - mix test (default) â†’ 309 tests + 1 doctest, 0 failures, 13 excluded
  - mix test --include e2e_slow â†’ 322 tests + 1 doctest, 0 failures, 2 skipped (matches IMPLEMENTED report)
- Audit-expanded scope (per step 11): none â€” no prior AUDIT: BOUNCE comments on this ticket.

Verdict: PASS

Notes (advisory only):
- Spec r4 wording vs implementation behaviour: r4 (post-amendment) says "site is classified `:timeout` regardless of which phase actually cleared the task", but `MutationLoop.cancel_on_timeout/2` honours `{:ok, {:ok, result}}` from the graceful phase as a completion (â†’ `:survived`/`:killed`). The graceful-cancel test at :541 asserts `:survived`, consistent with the implementation. The decision body and loop comments explain the choice (clean grace-window return â†’ result is honored). This is a deliberate design but the spec sentence reads as if classification is always `:timeout` â€” the reader has to follow the implementation/decision comments to learn the carve-out. Consider tightening r4's wording in a follow-up to "If the task returns a clean result inside the grace window, classification follows the result; otherwise `:timeout`."
- mutation_runner.ex:340-342 comment claims `:code.purge/1` is "Safe in the non-timeout / graceful-cancel paths too" but `settle_code_server!/3` only invokes purge on `{:timeout, _}` outcomes (and the test at :658 pins that gate). The "safe in non-timeout paths too" remark is true but unused; it could mislead a future reader. Cosmetic only.
- Golden delta is minimal and explained: top-level `warnings.count` 1 â†’ 0 for scenario 4 because the prior brutal-kill leaked into the snapshot growth. No other fields shifted, kill_rate identical.

AUDIT: APPROVE (auto â€” selective audit, orchestrated)

title: DISCOVERY: MutationLoop Task.shutdown(:brutal_kill) corrupts Code.Server load locks
